title: The Fading Promise of Higher Education
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/jay-tolson
text:
What is a university supposed to do? For much of the long history of this institution, beginning with the cathedral schools of medieval Europe, the mission was relatively clear. But things have grown more complicated in recent decades. In his 1963 book, The Uses of the University, Clark Kerr, architect of California’s modern public university system, declared that higher education had outgrown the old ideal types represented by the “academic cloister of Cardinal Newman” and the “research organism of Abraham Flexner”—alluding, respectively, to the great defender of the Oxford system and the twentieth-century reformer who remodeled American universities on a German, research-based model. Today, we ask much more of institutions of higher education, perhaps more than any other society ever has.

Beyond the already outsized tasks of educating young people and performing invaluable research, universities in America run investment funds and hospitals, field what are essentially minor-league teams in several sports, and provide the kind of job training that companies no longer offer. We count on them to emancipate young people from their parents and initiate them into adulthood—and, though we rarely say so explicitly, we still expect them to provide their charges with a moral and political education. Certainly, we are upset when they appear to fail to do so.

Perhaps it is not surprising that we now hear talk on all sides about a crisis of public faith in our universities. There is a sense that the relations between university and society, contentious at least since the nineties (and perhaps since the fifties or sixties), have now become bitterly hostile.

A sign of the scale of the crisis is that many within the walls of the university are now prepared to agree that something is wrong. A widely discussed report released in April by a committee at Yale University pointed to three forces undermining public confidence in higher education, which fell from 57 percent in 2015 to just 36 percent in 2024: the high and rising cost of attendance, concerns over the admissions process, and criticism of “what is said and taught on university campuses, including matters of free speech, political bias, and self-censorship.” 

For a thousand years, universities have been sustained by the communities and nations in which they are embedded. In his essay for The Fading Promise of Higher Education, our thematic section, Benjamin S. Bernard, a Yale University historian and a contributor to his institution’s report, reminds us that university-society relations have broken down countless times—often in ways tied to broader technological, cultural, and political shifts that threaten to make academics into hidebound defenders of a vanishing status quo. Calibrated concessions by scholars, even when they come late, have ensured the institution’s longevity. Might embracing greater “epistemic humility” allow them to do so again now?

The first recommendation of the Yale report was for universities to accept their share of responsibility for the declining public confidence. But is the hour too late for mea culpas? The Trump administration, motivated by critics of higher education’s liberal drift, has terminated as much as $8.2 billion in grants to universities and withheld funds from universities such as Harvard, UCLA, Columbia, and Duke, charging them with promoting antisemitism, indoctrinating their students, and rigging the admissions process along racial lines. The speed with which many storied institutions have capitulated under pressure—shuttering diversity programs, scrubbing mentions of gender from their online materials, and adopting, overnight, a strenuously neutral tone in their internal communications, after a decade of activism-inflected emails from high-level administrators accompanying every political-cultural flash point, has surprised and dismayed many liberals, while cheering conservatives.

Serious as the political threats are, higher education confronts perils that go far beyond the slings and arrows of the Department of Justice. A decrease in the number of international students—also a result, in significant measure, of Trump-administration policy—adds to the demographic factors shrinking the number of students in college classrooms. These are already forcing many institutions to close their doors (as Hampshire College, in Massachusetts, recently announced it would) or take an axe to majors that don’t bring in money (which invariably means the humanities).

Meanwhile, the rise of artificial-intelligence-powered chatbots is degrading the pedagogical environment on college campuses, making it easy for students to shirk reading and instructors to shirk grading. Little wonder that more and more people are concluding that college education is an expensive scam. In “Artificial Negligence,” writer and comparative literature scholar Dennis M. Hogan shows that university administrators are keen to adopt the technology, despite outcries from faculty, on the grounds that it represents a chance to cut costs, something students and parents want. But a closer look at the real reasons that college tuition keeps getting more expensive, he writes, suggests administrators’ real motives may be less honorable.

An under-acknowledged cause of higher education’s plight is the gradual abandonment—or outright betrayal—of public universities’ democratic commitment to educating the people of their states. In “How America’s Public Universities Stopped Being Public,” economist Marshall Steinbaum and historian David Austin Walsh praise the marvel that was the American public university in the age of the GI Bill, and relate how changes in higher education’s funding model, now heavily reliant on student loans, created a market that rewarded schools for selling out their own young people in favor of deep-pocketed students from out of state. And as public universities find ever more ingenious ways to lure students from privileged backgrounds and channel poorer ones to less desirable majors, Steinbaum and Walsh write, these one-time engines of egalitarianism now contribute to growing inequality.

Another consequence of the waning of American higher education’s golden age is the dramatic decline in tenure-track jobs in the humanities. We now have a generation of scholars who feel as though they are taking religious vows just as the monasteries are closing. Their predicament informs philosopher Mary Townsend’s essay, “Who Wants to Be a Professor?,” which examines the promises—false and true—of professorship, an office that represents an authority we know to be almost extinguished, ever more so with the increasing “casualization” of academic labor, and yet which continues, somehow, to inspire a desire for learning no chatbot can quite replicate.

It was Clark Kerr who coined the term “multiversity” to describe the multiplying roles that higher education plays in contemporary society. Ousted from his perch at the University of California in 1967 by then Governor Ronald Reagan in revenge for the Free Speech Movement protests at Berkeley, Kerr was also the first to suffer the fate that presidents at Harvard, Columbia, and the University of Virginia have recently met, casualties of the culture war over American universities. Kerr failed to anticipate how the expanded responsibilities of the university would make it vulnerable even as it became more powerful, something that succeeding generations of administrators have likewise struggled to grasp. Educators may take some consolation in the fact that, at least in the short term, the university remains indispensable to society for the many roles it plays—as a leader in scientific, medical, and other critical research, as a vital contributor to local and national economies, as a (controversial) sponsor of semi-professionalized sports, as a shaper of elite culture, and, at its increasingly rare best, as a repository and transmitter of humane values, without which the moral and intellectual life of the nation would be even more impoverished. Looked at from the other direction, higher education’s crisis is America’s to resolve, and it will be America’s great loss if the effort fails.

* * *

How much the United States owes to the life of the mind—from its inception to the present—is clearly evident in the three essays of our print symposium on the two-hundred-fiftieth anniversary of the American Revolution. Each of the essays in Aspects of 1776 illustrates the importance of deep literacy to those Colonial upstarts of the Founding era, not simply the habits of reading and writing but the informed critical thinking that was shaped by those habits.

That value comes through most conspicuously in Nicholas Carr’s “The Revolutionary Post,” his historically informed paean to an institution formally established by the Continental Congress one year before the Revolution but germinating well before 1775, as British Americans increasingly felt the need to relate their experiences, grievances, and ideas to their fellow Colonials up and down the Atlantic seaboard. The crucial figure was Benjamin Franklin, who went from being deputy postmaster of the parliamentary post, whose services and infrastructure he thoughtfully expanded, to assuming leadership of the US Post Office. As Carr appreciatively observes, “Like many other American Founders, Franklin was committed to the Enlightenment ideal of communal, free-flowing thought, and his overhaul of the postal system takes on a deeper meaning when viewed in light of his humanistic beliefs. It was more than a pragmatic effort to enhance the efficiency and reach of a governmental service. It was an attempt to turn America itself into a republic of letters.”

Although a fierier figure in the Founding pantheon, Thomas Paine was another major contributor to that literate republic, crusading relentlessly for the radical egalitarian vision handed down from his Leveller forebears in his native England. Brooke Allen, in “To Begin the World Over Again,” deftly navigates both the shrewdness and overreaching idealism of the author of Common Sense: “He came to America on the eve of the Revolution, with a letter of introduction from Benjamin Franklin, whom he had met in London, and began what would quickly become a dazzling career as a pamphleteer, newspaper writer, and propagandist of genius: Common Sense (1776) made him perhaps the greatest ‘influencer’ in our nation’s history.”

In “Representing the American Revolution,” Martha Bayles offers an incisive critical survey of some of the more notable efforts to do just that—in popular and scholarly histories as well as in stage productions, films, and documentaries. Representing the Revolution in these politically volatile times is particularly challenging, but Bayles has high praise for the many who have done it well, including Ken Burns in his PBS documentary: “Like the tragic compromises that locked in slavery for another half century, the brutal conquest of the American Indians is impossible to celebrate. But the series does not claim that these evils outweigh the goods that were achieved, and neither does it claim the obverse. In these parlous times, such evenhandedness is, to say the least, refreshing.”

Do we yet understand the full significance of the American Revolution, even from this distant prospect? Probably not. An apocryphal account has the former Chinese Premier Zhou Enlai responding to a question about the significance of the French Revolution with brusque certitude: “Too early to say.” In fact, he was likely referring to the French student riots of 1968. If the apocryphal version lives on, it is because it underscores the difficulty of rendering final judgments on important historical moments even from a great temporal remove. The factors informing the perceptions of the observers—whether historians, artists, or filmmakers—are constantly changing. Representations of 1776, Bayles shows, reveal just as much about the spirit of our times as they do about that of the Founding era. And one thing the best of these representations share is an awareness of the precariousness of the legacy the Founders entrusted to us.
